coredata.dc:title: Preparation of medium-long wavelength aggregation aggregation-induced emission carbon dots induced by nitrogen and sulfur doping

Zhao, Zhongguo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are extensively employed across multiple fields due to their exceptional optical properties, stability and other advantages. However, the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon has impeded the development of CDs in solid-state luminescence applications, including anti-counterfeiting and color display devices. In this study, we synthesized multicolor solid-state fluorescent CDs via the solvothermal reaction of dithiosalicylic acid with three different amine molecules, using acetic acid as the solvent. The resulting CDs demonstrate dual fluorescence emissions in solution, with the long-wavelength emission exhibiting typical aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics. In the solid state, the synergistic effects of enhanced sp2 conjugation domains and increased graphitic nitrogen on the surfaces of the CDs result in a red shift in fluorescence. The AIE phenomenon is achieved by restricting the intramolecular motion of the disulfide -containing bonds present on the surfaces of the CDs. Utilizing their unique dual emission properties, excellent AIE characteristics and tunable fluorescence colors. These CDs are intended for applications in information anti-counterfeiting, fingerprint detection and optoelectronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Dual emission
Long wavelength
Information encryption applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the F-CDs (a), U-CDs (b) and C-CDs (c). (upper right) are high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images, the illustrations (lower right) are particle size distribution. SEM images of the F-CDs (d), U-CDs (e) and C-CDs (f). (g) X-ray diffraction patterns of CDs. (h) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (i) XPS full spectrum of CDs.
(a–c) High-resolution C 1s, (d–f) High-resolution O 1s, (g–i) High-resolution N 1s and (j–l) High-resolution S 2p spectrum of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs.

Normalized UV–Vis absorption, FL excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of F-CDs powder (a), U-CDs powder (b) and C-CDs powder (c). (d–f) PL emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs powder, respectively. Fluorescence decay curve of F-CDs solution and powder (g), U-CDs solution and powder (h) and C-CDs solution and powder (i).

PL emission spectrum of the F-CDs (a), U-CDs (b) and C-CDs (c) powder dissolved in different solvents under 365 nm excitation. Photographs of the F-CDs (d), U-CDs (e) and C-CDs (f) solution with varying volume ratios of water (from 0 to 100 %) under sunlight (top) and 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (bottom). PL emission spectra of the F-CDs (a), U-CDs (b) and C-CDs (c) solution with varying ratios of water.
Application of F-CDs, U-CDs, C-CDs in anti-counterfeiting (a), multi-information encryption(b) and fingerprint recognition (c).

Physical diagram of (a)F-CDs, (b)U-CDs, (c) C-CDs LEDs working at 3.10V, CIE coordinate spectrum of (d)F-CDs, (e)U-CDs, (f) C-CDs LEDs, Electro emission spectra of (g)F-CDs, (h)U-CDs, and (i)C-CDs LEDs.
Diagram of the process of preparing F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs.
QY, τ, Kr and Knr of CDs solution and powder excited by 365 nm.

head.ce:title: Preparation of medium-long wavelength aggregation aggregation-induced emission carbon dots induced by nitrogen and sulfur doping

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are extensively employed across multiple fields due to their exceptional optical properties, stability and other advantages. However, the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon has impeded the development of CDs in solid-state luminescence applications, including anti-counterfeiting and color display devices. In this study, we synthesized multicolor solid-state fluorescent CDs via the solvothermal reaction of dithiosalicylic acid with three different amine molecules, using acetic acid as the solvent. The resulting CDs demonstrate dual fluorescence emissions in solution, with the long-wavelength emission exhibiting typical aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics. In the solid state, the synergistic effects of enhanced sp2 conjugation domains and increased graphitic nitrogen on the surfaces of the CDs result in a red shift in fluorescence. The AIE phenomenon is achieved by restricting the intramolecular motion of the disulfide -containing bonds present on the surfaces of the CDs. Utilizing their unique dual emission properties, excellent AIE characteristics and tunable fluorescence colors. These CDs are intended for applications in information anti-counterfeiting, fingerprint detection and optoelectronics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial derived from carbon-based materials. CDs have been extensively studied over the past decade as alternatives to traditional rare-earth luminescent materials, owing to their excellent optical properties and ease of preparation [1,2]. Thanks to their non-toxicity [3], excellent biocompatibility [4] and stability [5], CDs have rapidly advanced in recent years across various fields, including bioimaging [6,7], sensing [8], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [9,10] and information encryption [11]. In these applications, CDs typically need to be combined with various dispersants or solid matrices [12]. However, while many CDs exhibit excellent optical properties in solution, they suffer from the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon in the solid state [13]. Similar to organic fluorescent molecules, CDs can experience fluorescence quenching in the aggregated state due to intermolecular π-π stacking interactions or excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [14], which limits their applications in bioimaging [15], LED devices [16] and information encryption [17].

The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect, proposed by Tang and his team, is an effective strategy to overcome aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [18]. In recent years, many researchers have employed the AIE strategy to develop solid-state fluorescent (SSF) carbon dots (CDs). The resulting AIE CDs exhibit excellent solid-state fluorescence and can quickly precipitate as powders upon the addition of high viscosity solvents [19]. For instance, Hu and colleagues [20] synthesized yellow hydrophilic CDs with efficient solid-state luminescence using trimeric hexanoic acid and piperazine. These CDs displayed only weak yellow emission when well-dispersed in aqueous solution, but their luminescence was significantly enhanced in the solid state or in poor solvents. However, achieving multicolor tuning of AIE CDs remains a key challenge to meet the application requirements for multicolor SSF materials. Two important factors influencing the emission color of SSF CDs are the quantum size effect and heteroatom doping [21]. According to molecular orbital theory, as the conjugate size of CDs increases, the emission wavelength shifts red (indicating a smaller HOMO-LUMO gap energy) [22]. For example, Zhang et al. [23] synthesized multicolor fluorescent CDs by controlling the temperature during the solvothermal reaction. As the temperature increased, the size of the CDs gradually grew. As the degree of carbonization of CDs increases, the emission wavelength is red-shifted. Similarly, the number of functional groups on the surface of CDs and their positions within the structure can also induce a red shift [24]. For instance, Hu and his colleagues [12] adjusted the type of precursor to control the content of graphitic nitrogen in the structure of the CDs, achieving multicolor-emitting CDs. Additionally, the optical properties of CDs can be modulated by altering their aggregation state and through the doping of heteroatoms.

Based on the above problems and strategies, we synthesized CDs with yellow, orange and red fluorescent colors by one-step solvothermal method using dithiosalicylic acid with three molecular amines with different nitrogen atom contents as precursors and acetic acid as a solvent. These CDs showed typical AIE properties in poor solvents and dual fluorescence emission in solution. The XPS test and optical characterization proved that, in aggregated state, the redshift of CDs fluorescence originates from the increase of sp2 conjugated domains with the increase of graphitic nitrogen on the surface of aromatic ring CDs. While the AIE phenomenon is attributed to the inhibition of intramolecular movement of disulfide bonds on the surface of CDs, and the two together synthesize a series of multi-colored AIE CDs. On this basis, based on their AIE and dual emission properties in solution. These CDs were used in anti-counterfeiting, fingerprint detection and LED devices, demonstrating their great potential for applications in various fields.

The synthesis of CD was achieved by solvent heat treatment for 10 h followed by dialysis purification and drying. CDs with a unique symmetric molecular structure were prepared from dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and urea according to the method previously reported by our group [25]. Among them, the S–S bond in DTSA plays an important role in the synthesis of SSF CD. In order to tune their emission colors, we obtained multi-color luminescent solid CDs by adjusting the temperature as well as selecting molecular amines with different numbers of nitrogen atoms as precursors for the reaction. Depending on the increase of nitrogen atoms as well as the temperature, the emission colors of the CDs ranged from yellow to deep red at 365 nm (Fig. S1). The combined effect of nitrogen atoms and temperature is the key to produce long wavelength emission. Multicolor solid-state fluorescent CDs were prepared by using formamide (FA), urea (UR) and carbonyl hydrazine (CH) as reaction precursors. The yellow-emitting CDs from FA, the orange-emitting CDs from UR and the red-emitting CDs from CH were selected for detailed structural and optical characterization, considering the need to select the optimal emitting CDs for subsequent characterization. For convenience, these samples are labelled as F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs, where the prefixes indicate the corresponding precursors (Scheme 1 ).

The CDs were comprehensively analyzed in terms of structure and surface morphology using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). The TEM images shown in Fig. 1 a–c indicate that the F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs are all sub-globular in structure and well dispersed with almost no aggregation. The particle size distribution ranges from 1 to 4 nm for F-CDs, from 1 to 5 nm for U-CDs and C-CDs (inset). The average particle sizes of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs are 2.76 nm, 2.97 nm and 3.49 nm, respectively. The HR-TEM images (inset) show that all three CDs exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes, The inter-lattice distance is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of the graphite-like structure. [26]. From the results, it can be seen that the grain size of the CDs increases with increasing temperature, which suggests that the quantum size effect plays a key role in the redshift of the photoluminescence (PL) emission of the CDs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 1d–f) show that CDs are in multilayer stacking morphology in the aggregated state, and the size of the aggregates reaches micrometer scale. Meanwhile, the XRD patterns (Fig. 1g) show that F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs show a sharp conical diffraction peak at≈25.7° corresponding to the graphite (002) lattice plane, while a weak diffraction peak at≈39.5° corresponds to the (100) lattice plane [25]. Overall, the XRD patterns corroborate each other with the results observed via TEM, suggesting the presence of a graphite-like stacked structure in the CDs.

Next, the surface functional groups of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 1h, where the broader absorption band located at 3120 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H/N–H stretching vibration [27], the absorption peak located at 1680 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching motion of the CO bond. The absorption peak located at 1419 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–N bond, the presence of the C–S bond located at 1267 cm−1 vibrations. The out-of-plane bending vibration of the C–H bond on the benzene ring at 905 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the S–S bond at 536 cm−1 were also observed [19,26,28]. The above results indicate that the synthesized carbon-dot CDs consist of conjugated aromatic rings containing C, O, N and S elements. In particular, it was found by comparison that the infrared absorption peak intensities of the N–H and CN groups of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs were not identical, which was mainly due to the different doping contents of N atoms resulting from the three precursors. The chemical compositions of the CDs were probed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectra (Fig. 1i) showed four peaks at 164.3, 284.5, 408.2 and 532.1 eV for the F-CDs, 157.1, 279.1, 392.1 and 525.1 eV for the U-CDs and 163.1, 284.2, 400.7 and 532.9 eV for the C-CDs. This indicates that the three CDs are composed of four elements, namely, C, N, O and S. The contents of C, H and O are basically the same, whereas the content of N varies due to the number of nitrogen atoms contained in the raw materials, which indirectly lead to CDs fluorescence color change.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the high-resolution C 1s spectra reveal three peaks (Fig. 2a–c) corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O and CO [29]. It is worth noting that with the red shift of the fluorescence emission of CDs, the content of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms at 284.8 eV (CC/C–C) increases (Table S1). The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 2d–f) indicate the presence of oxygen in both CO (531.5 eV) and C–O (533.0 eV) environments [30]. The N 1s spectra for F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs display three component peaks at 399.5, 400.7 and 398.7 eV, which correspond to pyridinic N, graphitic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [31] (Fig. 2g–i). Specifically, the ratio of pyridine nitrogen from F-CDs to C-CDs decreased from 59 % to 18 %, while the proportion of graphitic N increases from 40 % to 60 % (Table S1). This suggests that nitrogen-containing groups play a significant role in inducing luminescence in CDs. The increase of nitrogen content in the product leads to a red shift in the absorption spectrum of CDs, thereby reducing the band gap energy of CDs, which in turn leads to a red shift in the emission wavelength [32]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2j–l) displays two peaks corresponding to C–S (163.1 eV), S–H (163.4 eV), and S–S (164.4 eV) [33]. Collectively, these four high-resolution spectra indicate the successful incorporation of sulfur and nitrogen atoms into the structures of the three types of CDs. Due to the non-rigid nature of the S–S bonds, the surface functional group of the CDs can rotate around these bonds. In addition, the increase in graphitic nitrogen caused a red shift in the fluorescence wavelength of CDs.

To evaluate the optical properties of CDs, we investigated the UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission characteristics of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs in both powder and solution forms. As shown in Fig. 3 a–c, the PL spectra of the F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs powders exhibited distinct variations, each featuring two luminescence centers. The fluorescence emission spectra revealed a strong peak at 490 nm and a weaker peak at 590 nm for F-CDs, while U-CDs and C-CDs displayed emission peaks at 490 nm and 630 nm, respectively. With the increasing nitrogen content in the amine precursors, the intensity of the red fluorescence peaks gradually increased relative to the green peaks, accompanied by a noticeable change in the fluorescence color of the CDs. C-CDs exhibited only a small green emission peak. The excitation spectra indicated that the dual emissions in F-CDs were excited at 365 nm and 470 nm, whereas U-CDs and C-CDs showed excitations at 365 nm and 560 nm, respectively. The normalized UV–vis absorption spectra demonstrated strong absorption across the 200–650 nm range for the CDs powders, attributed to various chromophore groups present in the CDs. The absorption peak near 320 nm is linked to the π-π∗ transitions of CC bonds [34], while the absorption bands at 450–575 nm correspond to n-π∗ transitions occurring in the surface states of CO, C–O, C–S and S–S structures [26,35]. The intensity of these absorption bands increased sequentially from F-CDs to C-CDs, which may be related to the increasing presence of CN groups in the CDs structure, suggesting that the graphitic nitrogen content contributes to the redshift of the emission wavelength [36]. The UV–vis spectra for the solutions of the three types of CDs exhibited an absorption peak at 350 nm, attributed to the π-π∗ transitions of CC (Fig. S2). The prepared CDs solutions displayed blue PL characteristics, with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths around 370 nm and 450 nm, respectively. In solution, the fluorescence of the CDs was attributed to ordinary carbon core luminescence, resulting from the π-π∗ transitions and the extensive conjugated structure of the CDs. Conversely, the intensity of the absorption band in the 450–575 nm range nearly disappeared. This suggests that CDs exhibit carbon core luminescence only in the short wavelength region. Surface fluorescent molecules containing S–S bonds do not fluoresce in dilute solutions.

Fig. 3d–f represents the PL emission of F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs powders at different excitation wavelengths. In the F-CDs system, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 nm to 480 nm, the emission peak of F-CDs always remained around 500 nm, showing the excitation-independent property. This means that F-CDs dominate the long-wavelength emission in the solid state, and their fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength [20]. In the systems of U-CDs and C-CDs, the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 nm to 500 nm. The emission peaks of both U-CDs and C-CDs were double emission peaks dominated by the long wavelength of 600 nm and 620 nm, and the emission peaks gradually shifted to the direction of the long wavelength with the increase of excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity was also gradually increased.

Then, the time-resolved PL decay curves of these samples in the powder and solution states were measured under 365 nm excitation, and the results are shown in Fig. 3g and h. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was calculated using Eq (1), (2):
(1)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp

−
τ
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
−
τ
τ 2
)
(2)
τ avg
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The fitting of the fluorescence lifetime resulted in the fitting table shown in Table 1 . The average fluorescence lifetimes of F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs powders are 1.08 ns, 0.88 ns, and 0.29 ns, respectively. The average fluorescence lifetimes of F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs solutions are 2.90 ns, 2.68 ns, and 3.25 ns, respectively. The radiative jump ratios (Kr) and non-radiative jump ratios (Knr) of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs in the powder and solution states were calculated using Formulas (3) and (4), and as shown in Table 1.
(3)
K nr
=
1 − Q Y
τ avg
(4)
K nr
=
1 − Q Y
τ avg

The Kr/Knr in the solution state was lower than that in the solid state. The significant reduction of non-radiative jump channels of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs in the powder state was verified. Accordingly, it was hypothesized that aggregation could inhibit the surface group motion and prevent the energy dissipation, thus suppressing the non-radiative leap process and significantly improving the PL efficiency of CDs [37]. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yields of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs in the powder and solution states were tested. The results show that the quantum yields of the three CDs in the solution state are much lower than the fluorescence quantum yields in the powder state, which is different from the low fluorescence quantum yields of other CDs in which the ACQ effect occurs in the solid state [38].

To further investigate the luminescence properties of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs in solvents. F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs were dissolved in n-hexane (HH), toluene (MB), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), anhydrous ethanol (ETH), glacial acetic acid (HAC), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), N, N-dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and deionized water (H2O) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. These eight organic solvents, ranging in polarity from small to large, did not show a significant red shift in fluorescence color under 365 nm irradiation (Fig. S4). The PL spectra of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs dispersed into solvents of different polarities were tested separately, and it was seen that the emission peaks of the three CDs did not show a regular redshift with the increase of solvent polarity under 360 nm excitation (Fig. 4 a–c). This indicates that the emission of the synthesized CDs is insensitive to the polarity of the solvent and does not exhibit solvent-induced discoloration of the PL. In general, solvent discoloration produces photoluminescence due to the interaction of its surface functional groups with the solvent, whereas the emission due to the carbon core state is not affected by the solvent [33]. The PL emission peaks did not exhibit a regular redshift. Instead, they corresponded to the dispersion of CDs in the solvent (Figs. S4a–c). This variation in fluorescence colors, observed under 365 nm UV irradiation, was due to the different solubility of CDs in various solvents. The solution basically shows blue fluorescence when the dispersion state is good. While the solution shows yellow, orange and red fluorescence when the dispersion state is poor due to the aggregation of CDs F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs, respectively. From Fig. 4a–c, it can be seen that the luminescence peaks are located around 400 nm when the dispersion is good and around 600 nm when the dispersion is poor. For example, in C-CDs solution, especially in aqueous solution, the initial blue luminescence center almost disappeared, and the intensity of the best emission peak at 600 nm was enhanced, and the solution showed bright red fluorescence. The above results indicate that the different aggregation states of CDs lead to changes in fluorescence color. These changes in fluorescence properties may be the result of the dual emission centers in CDs in conjunction with the AIE effect.

Based on the above experimental results, water and ethanol were selected as poor and good solvents, respectively, to further explore the AIE characteristics of CDs. The CDs powders were dispersed into different concentrations of aqueous solutions, as shown in Fig. 4d–f. Which show the photographs of the prepared F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs solutions under fluorescent lamps and 365 nm UV irradiation, in which the volume ratio of water varied from 0 % to 100 % (the other group was divided into anhydrous ethanol). The color gradually shifted from light blue to the color of the CDs powder itself as the water concentration increased. When the water volume fraction is less than 50 %, it shows blue light at 365 nm. In the case of water content greater than 50 %, it transformed from a clear liquid to an opaque liquid with suspended particles, which led to the luminescence of CDs itself. Further PL spectra of F-CDs, U-CDs and C-CDs were tested for solutions at different concentrations, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4g–i. Where the color of the solutions showed a red shift as the water volume ratio increased. The results show that CDs in dilute solutions exhibit similar optical properties as in the liquid state. While in aqueous solutions, the stronger hydrophobicity of CDs caused intermolecular aggregation of CDs. Overall, in good solvents, the carbon core emission caused by the CC group shows blue fluorescence, and the AIE molecules with flexible structure on the surface of the carbon dots do not emit light due to energy dissipation caused by intramolecular motion [39]. In contrast, in poor solvents, aggregation of carbon dots occurs. The carbon core, due to its rigid structure, undergoes intermolecular π-π stacking, which causes the short-wavelength blue fluorescence to burst [37,40]. Meanwhile, the high viscosity of the undesirable solvent leads to the restricted intramolecular rotation of CDs, causing them to show long-wavelength fluorescence.

Based on the above analysis, the possible luminescence mechanism of CDs was speculated. The double emission of F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs has different luminescence centers, which are derived from the sp2-conjugated carbon nuclei and AIE molecules attached to the S–S bond-containing on the surface of the CDs, respectively. In the solution state, the intramolecular rotation of the AIE molecules around the S–S group depletes the energy, causing the long-wavelength fluorescence burst. However, when F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs are aggregated, the π-π stacking of carbon nuclei with large conjugated structures causes the short-wavelength fluorescence to burst, and thus the blue emission is suppressed. In contrast, when CDs are aggregated in water or formed into powder, the intramolecular rotation is restricted, and the absorbed energy is converted to fluorescence by radiative transition, resulting in long-wavelength emission and enhanced SSF emission. Meanwhile, with the increase in the content of sp2 conjugated domains and nitrogen atoms, aromatic groups and graphitic nitrogen synergize to form local electronic states on the surface of CDs, leading to a significant redshift in their absorption and emission.

Considering that C-CDs emit blue fluorescence in ethanol and strong red fluorescence in water, this provides an idea for exploring their potential application as dual-switch ink in anti-counterfeiting and encryption. Qualitative acid-free filter paper was cut into a “window flower" shape, and the prepared C-CDs solution was applied to the filter paper using a pipette, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Under 365 nm UV light, the filter paper exhibits noticeable red fluorescence. When anhydrous ethanol was applied to the filter paper surface, the red fluorescence disappeared, and weak blue fluorescence appeared. After the ethanol evaporated, water was applied again, causing the blue fluorescence to vanish and the red fluorescence to reappear, thus the stimulation response of dual fluorescence emission is realized. Based on the excellent dual fluorescence emission characteristics and AIE effect of CDs, we have developed a dual-emission information encryption method. Its decryption method is similar to the Moss cipher, but it has a unique encryption method. As shown in Fig. 5b. Data encryption is divided into two steps. C-CDs are arranged in a 3 × 3 matrix as the first step to encrypt information. Since CDs exhibit completely different fluorescence in ethanol and aqueous solution, ethanol and aqueous solution are added to the 3 × 3 matrix as the second step of information encryption. The blue fluorescence after the combination of ethanol solution and C-CDs was used as one of the decryption information, and the red fluorescence after the combination of aqueous solution and C-CDs was used as another decryption information. Therefore, only when ethanol and aqueous solution are in the correct position of the matrix can the required “output” matrix be generated. There are nearly hundreds of possible encryption matrices (i.e., 2^9 = 512, where “2” denotes three solvents and “9” denotes nine positions in the matrix), and only one matrix can decrypt the data. Combining the dual-emission encryption method with the Moss cipher, the correct output information “S, K, G” is decrypted. The above findings clearly illustrate that the proposed dual-emission encryption strategy uses a simple 3 × 3 matrix to achieve a high level of data encryption security.

Additionally, taking advantage of the high quantum efficiency and lipophilic luminescent properties of CDs, they can be applied in fingerprint detection. The prepared F-CDs, U-CDs, and C-CDs powders were uniformly dispersed on weighing paper. A fingerprint pattern containing CDs was then imprinted on adhesive tape with a finger. The resulting fingerprint template is shown in Fig. 5c Under visible light, the fingerprint color appears similar with no noticeable difference. However, under 365 nm UV irradiation, the fingerprint is successfully observed on the transparent tape, showing three distinctly contrasting fluorescent colors: yellow, orange, and red.

At the same time, the prepared CDs also have excellent stability. As shown in Fig. S6a, the TGA curve of U-CDs shows that the carbon content only loses 0.24 % of its weight when the temperature reaches 100 °C. When the temperature reaches 150 °C, the carbon content only loses 0.39 % of its weight. It shows that U-CDs still maintain its excellent thermal stability at 150 °C. The thermal stability was determined by comparing the fluorescence emission spectra of U-CDs powders at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S6c, with the increase of temperature to 353 K, the emission intensity of U-CDs only decreased slightly, and the performance of the original sample was still retained by 62 %, which further verified the excellent thermal stability of U-CDs. In addition, CDs were irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp. The light stability was judged by comparing the fluorescence emission spectra of different irradiation time. As shown in Fig. S6b, the emission intensity of U-CDs only decreased slightly with the irradiation time increasing to 300 min, and still retained 86 % of the performance of the original sample, indicating that U-CDs had excellent photostability. The above tests show that the prepared CDs powder has excellent thermal stability and light stability, and has excellent practical application prospects.

CDs with excellent thermal stability and light stability can be used to explore LED optoelectronic devices. The prepared F-CDs/AB gel, U-CDs/AB gel and C-CDs/AB gel were combined with UV light chips to fabricate LEDs. Fig. 6 a–c shows the illumination images of the LED devices encapsulated with the three types of CDs/AB gel under operation. It is evident that the LEDs emit yellow, orange and red light continuously, demonstrating good electroluminescent performance in working condition. Fig. 6d–f displays the CIE color coordinates for the three LEDs. The chromaticity coordinates for the yellow, orange and red LEDs are located at (0.42, 0.53), (0.49, 0.49) and (0.54, 0.40), respectively. With correlated color temperatures (CCT) of 3918K, 2895K and 1860K, and a gradually increasing trend in color rendering index (CRI). The data shows that CCT and CRI exhibit opposing trends, which is consistent with results from most LED devices encapsulated with carbon dots as phosphors. The electroluminescent fluorescence spectra for the three LEDs are shown in Fig. 6g–i. The emission peaks for the yellow, orange, and red LEDs exhibit a redshift trend, which aligns with their light colors. Therefore, this study provides a potential pathway for optimizing the display index and relative color temperature, two key chromatic parameters, for LED devices in practical applications.

Based on the above, SSF CDs with yellow, orange and red fluorescent colors were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method using dithiosalicylic acid and three molecular amines with different nitrogen atom contents as precursors. The CDs were known to have a graphite-like lamellar structure as revealed by TEM and XRD patterns. Optical property characterization illustrated that these CDs exhibited typical AIE properties in poor solvents and had dual fluorescence emission in solution. In the solid state, the synergistic effect of increased sp2 conjugated domains and increased graphitic nitrogen on the surface of the CDs resulted in a redshift of the CDs fluorescence. The AIE phenomenon was achieved by inhibiting the intramolecular motion of the disulfide bonds on the surface of the CDs. On this basis, based on their AIE and dual emission properties in solution. These CDs were used in anti-counterfeiting, fingerprint detection and LED devices, demonstrating their great potential for applications in various fields. Based on the excellent luminescence properties of the three SSF CDs with different fluorescence colors, a series of application experiments were carried out on them. These carbon dots were successfully applied to LED devices in combination with commercial phosphors, and excellent chromaticity index parameters were obtained. Which provided solid experimental support for the application of this series of materials in the field of LED devices. In summary, the SSF CDs prepared in this study have a broad development prospect in the fields of anti-counterfeiting identification, fingerprint detection and optoelectronics.
Reagents, preparation, characterizations, and are available in Supporting Information.

Shouwang Kang: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Data curation. Guoqiang Fan: Formal analysis. Liu Ding: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhongwei Shang: Formal analysis. Jialin Wang: Formal analysis. Ke Chen: Software. Yalou Zhao: Formal analysis. Haifeng Luo: Writing – review & editing. Zhongguo Zhao: Supervision, Project administration.
